screens.name: Songs index
screens.purpose: Let the user scan the existing chart library and open the newly published song.

screens.name: Song score detail
screens.purpose: Present the complete melody and piano score with chord symbols in the current notation surface.

ux_rules: Preserve the current Songs list hierarchy; the new entry should behave like every existing row.
Use musical form, measures, staves, and chord symbols as the detail-page hierarchy rather than adding dashboard containers.
Keep the approved neutral section labels and do not rename them to Verse, Chorus, or Bridge without evidence.
Keep automatic-transcription caveats in the song data, but do not duplicate them as repeated labels around every score system.
Retain visible loading and error feedback while alphaTab imports the MusicXML asset.
Legacy chord-only entries must remain on the current AlphaTex path.

components: existing loading and error state panels

screen_contexts.layout_intent: Keep the current single-column, width-aware list with title and artist on the left and compact musical metadata on the right.
interaction_risks: A long Korean title could crowd the metadata column at intermediate widths.
A new row could introduce redundant badges or labels not present on sibling rows.
notes: Baseline desktop and mobile screenshots show the current row pattern fitting without horizontal overflow.

screen_contexts.layout_intent: Use the full available pane width for notation while preserving the existing title hierarchy and responsive page gutter.
interaction_risks: The three-part 86-measure MusicXML score may be dense or clip at mobile widths.
Chord-chart-only rest suppression would damage the full score.
alphaTab may fail while importing the static asset.
notes: The current renderer already has loading and error treatments.
No new interactive control or destructive action is required.

constraints: Do not change page layout, colors, typography, or row composition.
Use the existing Song data and SongList path so static route generation includes the new entry.
Keep semantic Tailwind utilities and current Geist hierarchy; do not add global CSS variables or page-specific color tokens.
Keep LeadSheetRenderer as the client boundary and ScoreViewer/SongDetailPage as existing server composition.
Switch between MusicXML and AlphaTex based on an optional Song asset field rather than forking a second renderer.
Apply chord-chart-only rest hiding and track-name hiding only to legacy AlphaTex charts.
Use alphaTab's existing page layout and responsive scale; change display settings only if browser evidence shows clipping.
Preserve loading, ready, and error state semantics.
Apply Next.js App Router, shadcn semantic styling, and Geist guidance without importing new component or font systems.
v0 exploration is intentionally not used because the approved design preserves an existing surface rather than inventing a new UI.
open_decisions: Whether alphaTab's default MusicXML staff sizing already fits a 390px viewport; decide from post-build browser evidence.

critical_judgments: The sixth row is immediately scannable and consistent with sibling rows.
Title, artist, feel, section count, and system count fit the available width.
No redundant label or badge was added.
must_not_regress: Existing five song links and routes

critical_judgments: Melody, piano right hand, piano left hand, and chord symbols visibly render.
Representative first, middle, and final score systems use the available pane width without avoidable horizontal clipping.
The title or part labels do not redundantly repeat in the local score context.
No framework or alphaTab error overlay appears.

visual_constraints: Musical notation is the dominant visual object; avoid cards, badges, gradients, shadows, and decorative chrome.
Preserve four-measure system behavior where alphaTab's imported score allows it.
Use semantic foreground/background/border utilities and current white score paper.
Desktop and 390px mobile screenshots are mandatory after implementation.
Browser verification must explicitly check representative score systems, redundant labels, available-width fit, and error overlays.
